steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank completes an inference drawn from Oladosu et al.'s finding: economists' estimates vary by more than a factor of five within a single country and time frame. Because country-specific characteristics (oil import-export ratios) are constant within a given country, they cannot explain this within-country variation. The inference must identify what else could explain it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — controlling for variations in countries' oil import-export ratios may have obscured inconsistencies in economists' findings about the effects of oil shocks at national levels.: The finding is about within-country variation, not cross-country ratio differences. This reverses the logic and is off-topic. ✗
  • Choice B — economists' conventional measures of national economic activity may be insufficiently sensitive to the effects of oil shocks.: Measurement sensitivity doesn't explain why different studies of the same country and time frame produce wildly different estimates. ✗
  • Choice C — methodological discrepancies in studies of oil shocks may have contributed to economists' inability to provide a generalized model of oil shocks' effects on national economies.: If country-specific factors are constant within a country, then differences in how studies are conducted (methodology) is the only remaining explanation for within-country variation. This directly follows from the finding. ✓
  • Choice D — differences in oil import-export ratios from one country to another may account for more of the differences in the effects of oil shocks on those countries' economies than economists previously believed.: This brings back country-specific cross-country comparisons, which the finding explicitly rules out as the source of variation. ✗
C is correct.
